dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 4 responding for a call involving unconsciousness at an apartment in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Ryan, an EMT with the rescue squad. Can I get a brief description of what happened here?
He had a fall down the stairs and is unresponsive now. We called when he didn't wake up after a few minutes.

Can you respond to a verbal command, sir? Can you squeeze my finger for me? He has no verbal response but shows motor compliance when we touch his hand. He is unresponsive, but his eyes open slowly when tapped on the shoulder.
Eyes open bilaterally. Both pupils are equal and reactive, approximately 2 mm.
He has no verbal response to questions or commands.
When we pressed on his left hand, he extended his left arm. Motor response is 5 out of 6.
Airway is clear. He is breathing independently and no current airway compromise is present.
We need to insert a nasopharyngeal airway due to potential head trauma. Successfully inserted through the left naris on the first attempt.
ETCO2 capnography confirms proper airway placement with a reading of 40.
Equal bilateral chest rise. Respiratory rate is 18 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation is 95% on room air.
Pulse is 99 per minute. Strong and regular. Blood pressure is 166/100.
Skin is warm, dry, and no cyanosis. Peripheral pulses are strong on both sides.
Capillary glucose is 102. This is within normal limits.
Applying 4-lead ECG now. Results show a sinus rhythm with no ST-segment abnormalities.
Altered mental status is present with no talking or consistent orientation. GCS score is 9-Eye 3, Verbal 1, Motor 5.
Did he show any signs or symptoms before the fall? Any odd feelings or sensations?
He stumbled and then fell. Reached for the wall, then dropped from about 15 steps.
He has diabetes and hypertension. He's been on metformin and lisinopril.
Taking metformin and lisinopril regularly. Not sure about the last dose.
No known allergies.
Per our information, he lost consciousness after the fall, prompting the 911 call.
Is there a history of intoxication or substance use? Bystanders have no information to provide.
On a 0 to 10 scale, how would you rate any discomfort you're feeling right now?
It's a zero for me.
We found a 1-inch laceration on the back of his head. It is oozing slightly but not actively bleeding.
Capillary refill is under two seconds. Skin has normal coloration with no signs of jaundice or pallor.
Manual cervical spine immobilization being applied now. We'll secure with an X-collar and backboard.
Spinal immobilization completed. He is secured on the backboard now.
A, B, and C are stable. Airway is patent, breathing adequate, circulation present.
Field impression is possible concussion with intracranial injury. GCS is 9 but improving.
Spinal restriction is in place. Preparing for a log roll to complete immobilization.
Pulse has decreased to 95 beats per minute.
Respiratory rate is 16 per minute and regular.
Blood pressure is 176 over 100 now.
Oxygen saturation has decreased to 93% on room air.
Glucose is reading 0, which appears to be a possible device issue.
We'll continue with protocol 4-1 for trauma management. Preparing for transport.
Transport is en route to [COMPANY_NAME], a trauma center.
All straps are secure. Patient is immobilized and prepared for safe transport.
Upon arrival, a trauma care handover (PUTS) will be initiated due to GCS improvement to 10.
Patient is stable. Trauma team is notified.
All field procedures and documentation are transferred to the ED team upon arrival.
Patient secured and ready for transport.
Retained vital signs: pulse 95, respiratory rate 16, BP 176/100, saturation 93%.
No repeat airway or spinal procedures needed at this time. Patient is stable.
Pain remains at 0. No acute analgesia requested.
Past medical history of diabetes and hypertension with current meds of metformin and lisinopril consistent.
No changes in meds. Metformin and lisinopril are ongoing.
No allergies reported.
Only external injury is the head laceration. No other visible trauma present.
All transport gear in place. Patient is ready to be moved.
Pulse 95, Resp 16, BP 176/100, Saturation 93% remain unchanged.
All transport positioning and equipment are confirmed. Heavy lifting is minimized.
No changes in med use. Still taking metformin and lisinopril.
Patient secured and all field procedures completed. No denials for transport.
All equipment is in place. Transport departure is imminent.
All straps locked. C-spine secured. Patient set for movement.
Patient is locked on the backboard. Transport equipment is in check.
Pain remains at zero.
Present medical history confirms diabetes and hypertension. No new findings observed.
Final destination is [COMPANY_NAME]. Trauma hospital is aware.
All secured and locked. No movement allowed before arrival.
Transport equipment check complete. No issues reported.
Field impression remains possible head trauma with altered mental status.
All systems go. Final confirmation for transport movement is complete.
Upon arrival, patient will be brought directly to the trauma bay.
ED team has been alerted and is aware of current status and findings.
All field procedures and findings have been shared. Transfer of care is complete.
Patient is strapped, backboard secured. Movement ready.
Pain score continues to remain at 0.
Any food or drink before this event? Patient may have had dinner between 5 and 6.
No signs of abdominal distention or rigidity observed.
No rectal bleeding or signs of urinary urgency reported.
No neck masses. Pupils are still reactive and equal.
No bruises or rash beyond the laceration. No signs of abuse noted.
Nothing blocking nose or ears. Oral airway remains patent.
Symmetrical chest rise. Normal breath and heart sounds.
Final transport readiness confirmed. Locked and ready.
No further field actions required. Patient is ready to go.
Pulse 95, Resp 16, BP 176/100, Saturation 93% maintain.
Patient is ready to move. Direct transport to [COMPANY_NAME].
Transport is leaving now. All items on board.
Patient is locked for transport. All monitoring in place.
[COMPANY_NAME] trauma team is on standby with full knowledge of the patient's status.
No complications expected during transport. All protocols followed.
